ERR001.message: [ERR001] Invalid address: too many '@' symbols in '{value}'

help.description: The address contains more than one '@' symbol. Only one '@' is allowed to separate the user from the host.
help.solution: Remove the extra '@' symbols. Use a single '@' to separate user and host, then optionally a ':' to specify the port.
help.examples:
> sshr -r user@host@22:2200             !!! INVALID (two @)
> sshr -r user@@192.168.1.20             !!! INVALID (two @)
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200        ✓ VALID

> sshr -r <user>@<host>:<port>           correct syntax

For more details about -r syntax use:
> sshr --help register

ERR002.message: [ERR002] Invalid address: too many ':' symbols in '{value}'

help.description: The address contains more than one ':' symbol. Only one ':' is allowed to separate the host from the port.
help.solution: Remove the extra ':' symbols. Use a single ':' after the host to specify the port, or omit it to use the default port 22.
help.examples:
> sshr -r alice@host:22:3300             !!! INVALID (two :)
> sshr -r alice@host::2200               !!! INVALID (empty segment)
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200        ✓ VALID

> sshr -r <user>@<host>:<port>           correct syntax

For more details about -r syntax use:
> sshr --help register

ERR003.message: [ERR003] Invalid address: missing required parts in '{value}'

help.description: The address contains empty segments. None of the parts separated by '@' or ':' can be empty.
help.solution: Ensure every segment has a value. User, host, and port must all be present and non-empty.
help.examples:
> sshr -r alice@:2200                     !!! INVALID (empty host)
> sshr -r @192.168.1.20:2200             !!! INVALID (empty user)
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200        ✓ VALID

> sshr -r <user>@<host>:<port>           correct syntax

For more details about -r syntax use:
> sshr --help register

ERR004.message: [ERR004] Invalid address: ':' must come after '@' in '{value}'

help.description: The ':' appears before the '@'. The port separator must come after the user-host separator.
help.solution: Place '@' first to separate user and host, then ':' for the port. Never the other way around.
help.examples:
> sshr -r alice:host@2200                 !!! INVALID (wrong order)
> sshr -r alice:192.168.1.20@2200        !!! INVALID (wrong order)
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200        ✓ VALID

> sshr -r <user>@<host>:<port>           correct syntax

For more details about -r syntax use:
> sshr --help register

ERR005.message: [ERR005] Invalid address: '@' must come after ':' in '{value}'

help.description: The '@' appears after the ':'. This means the port was placed between the user and the host, which is incorrect.
help.solution: Always place ':' after '@'. The order must be user@host:port, never user:port@host.
help.examples:
> sshr -r user:2200@192.168.1.20          !!! INVALID (wrong order)
> sshr -r alice:3300@server              !!! INVALID (wrong order)
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200        ✓ VALID

> sshr -r <user>@<host>:<port>           correct syntax

For more details about -r syntax use:
> sshr --help register

ERR006.message: [ERR006] Invalid address: port must be a number
ERR006.tip: Use a numeric value for the port

help.description: The port value after ':' is not a number. SSH ports must be numeric values between 1 and 65535.
help.solution: Provide a numeric value for the port. If you omit the port entirely, port 22 will be used by default.
help.examples:
> sshr -r alice@server:abc                !!! INVALID (not a number)
> sshr -r alice@server:22b                !!! INVALID (not a number)
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200        ✓ VALID

> sshr -r <user>@<host>:<port>           correct syntax

For more details about -r syntax use:
> sshr --help register

ERR007.message: [ERR007] Alias already exists: '{name}' is already registered

help.description: The alias you entered already exists in the SSH config file. Each alias must be unique to avoid conflicts.
help.solution: Choose a different alias for this connection, or delete the existing one first if you want to reuse the alias.
help.examples:
> Alias: myserver                          !!! INVALID (already exists)
> Alias: myserver-2                        ✓ VALID
> Alias: myserver-backup                   ✓ VALID

For more details about -r syntax use:
> sshr --help register

help.description: You entered text when a number was expected. The menu only accepts numeric options.
help.solution: Enter the number corresponding to the option displayed on screen.
help.examples:
> Input: yes                               !!! INVALID (not a number)
> Input: two                               !!! INVALID (not a number)
> Input: 1                                 ✓ VALID (confirm)
> Input: 2                                 ✓ VALID (edit alias)
> Input: 0                                 ✓ VALID (cancel)

ERR009.message: [ERR009] Invalid selection: enter a number between 1 and {max}
ERR009.tip: Select a valid option from the list

help.description: The number entered is outside the valid range of available connections.
help.solution: Look at the numbered list and enter a number that matches one of the listed connections.
help.examples:
> Input: 0                                 !!! INVALID (out of range)
> Input: 6  (when only 5 exist)           !!! INVALID (out of range)
> Input: 3                                 ✓ VALID

For more details about -d syntax use:
> sshr --help delete

help.description: There are no SSH connections stored in the config file, so there is nothing to display.
help.solution: Register at least one connection using the --register flag before trying to list connections.
help.examples:
> sshr -l                                 !!! No connections yet
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200         ✓ Register first
> sshr -l                                 ✓ Now connections appear

For more details about -l syntax use:
> sshr --help list

ERR011.tip: Enter a valid value and try again

help.description: The value entered does not match the expected data type for this prompt.
help.solution: Pay attention to the prompt and enter a value of the correct type.
help.examples:
> Expected number, got: forty-two          !!! INVALID
> Expected number, got: 42                 ✓ VALID

ERR012.tip: Run with --help to see available commands

help.description: The flag or command entered does not match any registered command in the system.
help.solution: Use the --help flag to see the full list of valid flags and their usage.
help.examples:
> sshr --invalid-flag                     !!! INVALID (unknown flag)
> sshr --register                          !!! INVALID (use -r instead)
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200         ✓ VALID

Available commands:
  -r, --register    Register a new connection
  -l, -ll, --list   List connections
  -d, --delete      Delete a connection
  -e, --edit        Edit a connection
  -h, --help        Show help

> sshr --help                              Show all commands

ERR013.tip: Run with --help to see available commands

help.description: You ran sshr without any arguments. The program needs at least one flag to know what action to perform.
help.solution: Provide a valid flag to tell sshr what you want to do. Use --help to see available options.
help.examples:
> sshr                                    !!! No arguments
> sshr --help                              ✓ Shows available commands
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200         ✓ VALID command

ERR014.message: [ERR014] Wrong number of arguments for '{command}' command
ERR014.tip: Run with --help to see correct usage

help.description: The command requires a specific number of arguments, but a different amount was provided.
help.solution: Check the correct usage with --help and provide the exact number of arguments expected.
help.examples:
> sshr -r                                 !!! Missing arguments
> sshr -r alice@192.168.1.20:2200         ✓ Correct arguments

For more details about the command and its respective arguments use:
> sshr --help <command>

ERR015.message: [ERR015] Error code '{code}' not found: no matching error registered
ERR015.tip: Double-check the error code you entered and try again

help.description: The error code you entered does not correspond to any registered error in the system. The code may be misspelled, outdated, or not yet implemented.
help.solution: Verify the error code you are searching for. Make sure it follows the 'ERR' prefix followed by three digits (e.g., ERR001, ERR014).
help.examples:
> sshr --help ERR999                     !!! INVALID (code does not exist)
> sshr --help ERRABC                     !!! INVALID (malformed code)
> sshr --help ERR001

ERR016.message: [ERR016] Unknown command: '{command}' is not a recognized command
ERR016.tip: Run with --help to see available commands

help.description: The command you entered after --help does not match any registered command in the system.
help.solution: Use sshr --help to see the complete list of available commands and check the spelling.
help.examples:
> sshr --help regiser                     !!! INVALID (typo)
> sshr --help register                    ✓ VALID

> sshr --help                              Show all available commands

ERR017.message: [ERR017] SSH config file not found: '~/.ssh/config' does not exist
ERR017.tip: Create the config file manually or run 'sshr build'

help.description: No SSH config file was found to store or read aliases. SSHR uses the plain text file '~/.ssh/config' to manage SSH aliases and connections.
help.solution: First, verify whether the 'config' file exists inside '~/.ssh/', which is the default location for storing your aliases. If you do not use that folder, check the directory defined in '~/.config/sshr/config.ini' and confirm that a 'config' file exists inside that directory. If you cannot find the 'sshr' folder inside '~/.config/', it means you have not run 'sshr build' yet, the command that creates the full configuration structure, including the 'config' file. In that case, run it so SSHR can generate the required files automatically.
help.examples:
> ls ~/.ssh/config                       !!! INVALID (file not found)
> mkdir -p ~/.ssh && touch ~/.ssh/config  ✓ VALID
> sshr build                               ✓ VALID (creates it automatically)

For more details about the build command use:
> sshr --help build
